infobox.value: * [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_in_Belgium "Dutch in Belgium") * [Flemish Sign Language](/wiki/Flemish_Sign_Language "Flemish Sign Language") (legally recognized)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Vlaamse Gemeenschap*, pronounced [[ˈvlaːmsə ɣəˈmeːnsxɑp]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Vlaamse_Gemeenschap.ogg "File:Nl-Vlaamse Gemeenschap.ogg"))[[a]](#cite_note-3) is one of the three [institutional communities](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium") of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), established by the [Belgian constitution](/wiki/Belgian_constitution "Belgian constitution") and having legal responsibilities only within the precise geographical boundaries of the Dutch-language area and of the bilingual area of Brussels-Capital. Unlike in the [French Community of Belgium](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium"),[[3]](#cite_note-4) the competences of the Flemish Community have been unified with those of the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region") and are exercised by one directly elected [Flemish Parliament](/wiki/Flemish_Parliament "Flemish Parliament") based in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels").

The **Flemish Community** ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Vlaamse Gemeenschap*, pronounced [[ˈvlaːmsə ɣəˈmeːnsxɑp]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Vlaamse_Gemeenschap.ogg "File:Nl-Vlaamse Gemeenschap.ogg"))[[a]](#cite_note-3) is one of the three [institutional communities](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium") of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), established by the [Belgian constitution](/wiki/Belgian_constitution "Belgian constitution") and having legal responsibilities only within the precise geographical boundaries of the Dutch-language area and of the bilingual area of Brussels-Capital. Unlike in the [French Community of Belgium](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium"),[[3]](#cite_note-4) the competences of the Flemish Community have been unified with those of the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region") and are exercised by one directly elected [Flemish Parliament](/wiki/Flemish_Parliament "Flemish Parliament") based in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flemish_Community&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[State reforms in Belgium](/wiki/State_reform_in_Belgium "State reform in Belgium") turned the country from a unitary state into a federal one. Cultural communities were the first type of decentralisation in 1970, forming the Dutch, French and German Cultural Community. Later on, in 1980, these became responsible for more cultural matters and were renamed to simply "Community", the Dutch (Cultural) Community also being renamed to the Flemish Community. In the same state reform of 1980, the Flemish and Walloon Region were set up (the Brussels-Capital Region would be formed later on). In Flanders it was decided that the institutions of the Flemish Community would take up the tasks of the Flemish Region, so there is only one [Flemish Parliament](/wiki/Flemish_Parliament "Flemish Parliament") and one [Flemish Government](/wiki/Flemish_Government "Flemish Government").

## Legal authority

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flemish_Community&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Legal authority")]

Main article: [Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium")

Under the Belgian constitution, the Flemish Community has legal responsibility for the following:

* education (except for degree requirements, the minimum and maximum age of compulsory education, and teachers' pensions);
* culture;
* language legislation in monolingual Dutch-speaking municipalities;
* certain aspects of welfare, health care and child allowances;
* international cooperation in all areas of the competency of the Community.

As the Flemish Community's institutions ([parliament](/wiki/Flemish_Parliament "Flemish Parliament"), [government and ministry](/wiki/Flemish_Government "Flemish Government")) absorbed all competencies of the Flemish region, they became also competent for all regional policy areas, including:

* agriculture (although the bulk of this policy is determined by the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"));
* public works and regional economic development;
* local authorities;
* environment;
* mobility;
* energy (although nuclear energy remains on the federal level).

Members of the Flemish Parliament elected in the Brussels-Capital Region have no right to vote on Flemish regional affairs, only on community affairs, since affairs concerning their region are governed by the [Brussels Parliament](/wiki/Brussels_Parliament "Brussels Parliament"). Legally speaking, in the region of Brussel-Capital, the Flemish Community is responsible not for individual people but for Flemish institutions such as schools, theatres, libraries and museums. The reason is that no distinct sub-national status exists in Belgium. Yet, individuals living in Brussels can opt by their own choice for certain policies of the Flemish Community.

## Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flemish_Community&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Language")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Br%C3%BCssel_%28Ortstafel%29.jpg/250px-Br%C3%BCssel_%28Ortstafel%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Br%C3%BCssel_(Ortstafel).jpg)

Bilingual sign in Brussels

[Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") is the official language of the Flemish Community. Minorities speak [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language"), [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"), [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"), [Berber](/wiki/Berber_languages "Berber languages"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"). Though most of these groups are recent immigrants, since the Middle Ages, [Jews](/wiki/Jews_in_Belgium "Jews in Belgium") have formed the oldest minority to retain its own identity.

Compared with most areas in the Netherlands, the historical dialects of Flemish people still tend to be strong and particular to locality. Since the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") however, the influences of radio and television, and of a generally prolonged education, as well as the higher mobility for short trips or for moving towards farther localities, have resulted in a deterioration of the traditional 'pure' dialects, in particular amongst younger people. Some of the differences between the dialects are eroding, and mainly in localities or suburbs with a considerable influx from other areas, new intermediate dialects have appeared, with various degrees of influence by standard Dutch. In Dutch, these are often called *[tussentaal](/wiki/Tussentaal "Tussentaal")* ("in-between language", often used for near-standard Dutch interspersed with typical dialect aspects) or, rather derogatorily, *verkavelingsvlaams* (a mix of more or less "cleaned-up" dialects as heard in a newly built-up suburban area with people influenced by different dialects). More recently,[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] a number of local initiatives have been set up to save the traditional dialects and their diversity.

In Brussels, the local dialect is heavily influenced by French, both in pronunciation and in vocabulary. Nowadays, most Flemings in Brussels do not speak the local dialect. This is due in part to the relatively large numbers of young Flemings coming to Brussels, after a long period of many more others moving out while [French-speakers moved in](/wiki/Frenchification_of_Brussels "Frenchification of Brussels").

In certain municipalities along the border with the [Walloon](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia") and the Brussels-Capital regions, French-speakers enjoy "[language facilities](/wiki/Municipalities_with_language_facilities "Municipalities with language facilities")". These cover rights such as to receive official documentation in their own tongue. Similar facilities are enjoyed by Dutch-speakers in some Walloon municipalities bordering the Flemish Region, by German-speakers in two municipalities in the French language area of the Walloon Region, and by French-speakers in the territory of the [German-speaking community](/wiki/German-speaking_Community_of_Belgium "German-speaking Community of Belgium").
The geographical limitations of the communities require the French Community to ensure Dutch basic education in its municipalities with facilities for speakers of Dutch, and the Flemish Community to finance French schools in its municipalities with facilities.

## Flemish institutions in Brussels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flemish_Community&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Flemish institutions in Brussels")]

Where responsibilities of the Flemish Region can be devolved to the provincial level, no such equivalent exists in the [Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels-Capital_Region "Brussels-Capital Region"), which itself exercises many competencies for *territorial tasks* elsewhere assigned to the provinces. The community competencies (education, culture and social welfare) there, are exercised by the two affected institutional communities. The Flemish Community therefore established a local elected council and executive (the [Flemish Community Commission](/wiki/Flemish_Community_Commission "Flemish Community Commission") or 'VGC') to cater for *intermediate-level decision making & public services*. The VGC then recognised local, municipal institutions to take care of the purely local public service in these community areas (called *gemeenschapscentra* or community centres).

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flemish_Community&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Media")]

Flanders has an official radio and television broadcasting company, the *Vlaamse Radio en Televisieomroep* or [VRT](/wiki/Vlaamse_Radio-_en_Televisieomroep "Vlaamse Radio- en Televisieomroep") in Dutch. Since 1989, several private companies for region-wide radio and television broadcasting have become established. There are also so-called "regional" broadcast companies of which the range is limited to only smaller parts of the Flemish Region. The written press is dominated by a number of 'quality' dailies (such as *[De Tijd](/wiki/De_Tijd "De Tijd")*, *[De Morgen](/wiki/De_Morgen "De Morgen")* and *[De Standaard](/wiki/De_Standaard "De Standaard")*), several 'popular' dailies (such as *[Het Laatste Nieuws](/wiki/Het_Laatste_Nieuws "Het Laatste Nieuws")* and *[Het Nieuwsblad](/wiki/Het_Nieuwsblad "Het Nieuwsblad")*) and a large number of general and specialized magazines. [*[neutrality](/wiki/Wikipedia:Neutral_point_of_view "Wikipedia:Neutral point of view") is [disputed](/wiki/Talk:Flemish_Community "Talk:Flemish Community")*]

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flemish_Community&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Communauté flamande* [[kɔmynote flamɑ̃d]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Flämische Gemeinschaft* [[ˈflɛːmɪʃə ɡəˈmaɪnʃaft]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German").

## References

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flemish_Community&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: References")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** Since the Brussels-Capital Region is part of both the Flemish and [French Community of Belgium](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium"), it is not possible to give a definitive population figure. The Brussels-Capital Region has 1,208,542 inhabitants (as of 2019-1-1), of which some 10-20% could be seen as being part of the Flemish Community. Together with the Flemish Region which has 6,589,069 inhabitants (as of 2019-1-1), this gives an estimated 6.5 to 7 million inhabitants.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** ["VGT: een erkende taal | Departement Cultuur, Jeugd & Media"](https://www.vlaanderen.be/cjm/nl/cultuur/vlaamse-gebarentaal/vgt-een-erkende-taal). *www.vlaanderen.be* (in Dutch). Retrieved 2024-06-07.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** The parliament of the [French Community](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium") is distinct from the [Walloon Parliament](/wiki/Walloon_Parliament "Walloon Parliament"); this is more obvious for the parliament of the [German-speaking Community](/wiki/German-speaking_Community_of_Belgium "German-speaking Community of Belgium") because its much smaller territory is within the latter region.
